Name: N,N-Dimethylacetamide
Remarks: pKs' values in the presence of water were obtained by pKs' = (Es/59) - log c(HClO4)c(Me4NOH), where Es (in mV)is the difference between the half-neutralization potentials of HClO4 and Me4NOH at concentrations c(HClO4) and c(Me4NOH) at the instant of half-neutralization. pKs was obtained as pKs' extrapolated to zero water content. By a different method of extrapolation to zero water content,  pKs ~ 40 was obtained. See also *6.
Reference: L.N. Bykova and A.V, Novikov, Zh, Anal. Khim., 38, 1191 (1983); J. Anal. Chem. USSR, 38, 903 (1983).

Remarks: paH values in the mixtures of the acid and its conjugate base (R4N+salt) or the base and its conjugate acid (picrate or perchlorate) were measured, using a glass electrode calibrated in mixtures of HClO4(pKa=4.5) and Bu4NClO4. Name: Hexamethylphosphoric triamide
Remarks: As in *1, but pKa(DPhG)=14.5,instead of 17.2 in *1, was used as reference. The difference between the pKa(DPhG)values was due to the correction made in *1b for the incomplete salt dissociation. When pKa values of group A are to be compared with those of group B, this difference must be taken into account.  In the potentiometric titration of the base with HClO4, two potential jumps were observed at 50% and 100% neutralization points, showing the formation of stable B2H+. pKa and logKf(B2H+) were obtained by pKa = 0.5(pH25 + pH75) and logKf(B2H+) = 0.5(pH25 - pH75) - logC, respectively, where pH25 and pH75 are the pH at 25 and 75% neutralizations and C is the initial concentration of the base. Name: Triethylphosphine oxide
Remarks: pKa was obtained from the potential at half-neutralization of the base with HClO4. 1,3-Diphenylguanidine(DPhG, pKa = 17.2) served as reference. The linear response of the glass electrode, confirmed in 4 x 10-4 - 4 x 10-5 M HClO4, ,gave pKa(DPhG)=17.2 within the accuracy of ± 0.5. In the potentiometric titration of the base with HClO4, two potential jumps were observed at 50% and 100% neutralization points, showing the formation of stable B2H+. pKa and logKf(B2H+) were obtained by pKa = 0.5(pH25 + pH75) and logKf(B2H+) = 0.5(pH25 - pH75) - logC, respectively, where pH25 and pH75 are the pH at 25 and 75% neutralizations and C is the initial concentration of the base. Reference: B.I. Stepanov, B.A. Korolev and A.I. Bokanov, Zh. Obshch. Khim., 39, 316 (1969); J. Gen. Chem. USSR, 39, 295 (1969).

Remarks: The standard potential of the hydrogen electrode, determined in anhydrous HClO4  solutions, was +0.075 V vs. Ag/0.1M AgClO4(SL) and used as reference. pKa was obtained from the potentials of the hydrogen and/or the glass electrodes in0.01M HA-0.01M Et4Na, assuming the Nemstian response of the electrodes. Reference: J.F. Coetzee and R.J. Bertozzi, Anal. Chem., 43, 961 (1971).

Name: Perchloric acid
Remarks: Corrected for the effect of hydronium perchlorate (H3O+ClO4-). Based on the complete dissociation of HSbCl6.
Reference: R.L. Benoit and C. Buisson, Electrochim. Acta, 18, 105 (1973).

Name: Picric acid
Remarks: pKa was obtained from the -log[Pic-] vs. -logC plot (C: analytical concentration of HPic). Simple dissociation occurred at C< 0.08 M, but at higher concentrations more complicated equilibria were observed. (cf. P.M.P. Eller and J.A. Caruso, Can. J. Chem., 51, 448 (1973)).
Reference: M. Castagnolo, A. de Giglio, A. Dell'atti and G. Petrella, Can. J. Chem., 53, 1651 (1975).

Name: Picric acid
Remarks: The standard potential of the hydrogen electrode, determined in anhydrous HClO4  solutions, was +0.075 V vs. Ag/0.1M AgClO4(SL) and used as reference. pKa was obtained from the potentials of the hydrogen and/or the glass electrodes in0.01M HA-0.01M Et4Na, assuming the Nemstian response of the electrodes. Benoit and Pichet[73B] obtained -0.10 V vs. Ag/0.1M AgClO4 as the standard potential of the hydrogen electrode. If based on this value, the pKa value should become smaller by ca. 3 units.
Reference: J.F. Coetzee and R.J. Bertozzi, Anal. Chem., 43, 961 (1971).

Name: Pyridine
Remarks: The standard potential of the hydrogen electrode, determined in anhydrous HClO4  solutions, was +0.075 V vs. Ag/0.1M AgClO4(SL) and used as reference. pKa was obtained from the potentials of the hydrogen and/or the glass electrodes in0.01M HA-0.01M Et4Na, assuming the Nemstian response of the electrodes. Benoit and Pichet[73B] obtained -0.10 V vs. Ag/0.1M AgClO4 as the standard potential of the hydrogen electrode. If based on this value, the pKa value should become smaller by ca. 3 units.
Reference: J.F. Coetzee and R.J. Bertozzi, Anal. Chem., 45, 1064 (1973).

Name: Salicylic acid
Remarks: The solution of H2S2O7 was unstable.
Reference: J.F. Coetzee and R.J. Bertozzi, Anal. Chem., 43, 961 (1971).

Name: Sulfolane
Remarks: Emfs of two cells, glass electrode/HCl(SL)/AgCl,Ag and glass electrode/Bu4NOH(SL)/AgCl,Ag, were measured by varying the HCl and Bu4NOH concentrations. The results were used to determine the standard emf and pKs.
Reference: A.P. Kreshkov, N.Sh. Aldarova and B.B. Tanganov, Zh. Fiz. Khim., 44, 2089 (1970); Russ. J. Phys. Chem., 44, 1186 (1970).

Name: Trifluoromethanesulfonic acid
Remarks: Unpublished results by R. Meilleur and J. Charbonneau.
Reference: R.L. Benoit and C. Buisson, Electrochim. Acta, 18, 105 (1973).

Name: m-Chlorobenzenesulfonamide
Remarks: The completely hydrated glass electrode was used after calibration in buffer solutions of picric acid (pKa = 17.4) and benzoic acid (pKa = 26.3). The temperature of the solution was not given.
Reference: M. Ludwig, O. Pytela and M. Vecera, Collection Czechoslov. Chem. Commun., 51, 1948 (1986).
